turns.speaker: user
turns.text:
2 Background of the Study
A strong illustration of political will is the establishment of the enabling statute in Zimbabwe, which supports the favorable environment in which a dedicated disaster management agency was established. The development of the institutional structure, appropriate policy, and legal requirements is informed by the corporate commitment. According to the National Policy for Civil Protection, every citizen of this country should do everything they can to prevent or mitigate the effects of disaster. The Zimbabwe Civil Protection Act of 1989 states that local administrations are responsible for overseeing the effectiveness of national government-launched hazard reduction efforts through the relevant sector Ministries. The Department of Civil Protection is a government organization that is housed inside the Ministry of Local Government, Public Works, and Governmental Housing. The Department was established to manage disasters on a large scale by coordinating all necessary stakeholders. The department is run by a Director and a Deputy Director, with the assistance of officers, secretaries, typists, and a driver. The current system makes use of the already-existing public, commercial, and non-profit organizations, whose everyday operations comprise elements for catastrophe risk reduction and community development. The lack of a national-level plan policy, the absence of a central, state, or district institutional framework, poor inter-sectoral coordination, the absence of an early warning system, the slow response from relief organizations, the dearth of trained and dedicated search and rescue teams, and poor community empowerment are factors that have hampered the response to disasters in the past.
The effects of climate change are being felt by societies everywhere in the world in previously unheard-of ways. Felbermayr and Gröschl (2014) assert that these effects can lead to a 6.8% decrease in per-person GDP in the year they occur, while Klomp and Valckx (2014) explain how climate change negatively affects economic growth per person using 750 empirical estimations. According to Munich Re (2018), tropical cyclones were responsible for more than half of all natural disaster losses globally, with damages from these storms exceeding USD 2111 billion between 1980 and 2018. On a global scale, they are among the worst natural disasters (Kunze 2021). Furthermore, no part of the African continent has escaped damage, particularly the area south of the Sahara. Because of their geographic location, most countries are, if anything, more vulnerable to natural disasters (Klomp and Valckx 2014). According to Arndt et al. (2012), Mozambique’s average annual rainfall totals have fallen while the country’s temperatures are estimated to have increased by 0.6 °C between 1960 and 2009 as a result of climate change. More than 1.7 million people were impacted by two powerful cyclones that hit Mozambique in quick succession in March and April 2019. These storms resulted in losses and damages of USD 3 billion, and it is anticipated that recovery and reconstruction will cost USD 3.4 billion overall (Mozambican Government, 2019).
Zimbabwe has not been exempt from the massive destruction and displacement brought on by cyclones, which frequently create natural disasters such as tropical cyclones, droughts, and floods. It is projected that these dangers would become more frequent and severe as a result of climate change. Over half of all fatalities from natural disasters are caused by cyclone-induced flooding, making it one of the most common and deadly occurrences (Rana and Routray 2018; Mhlanga et al. 2019). In 2000, floods brought on by Cyclone Eline killed more than 700 people, left over 500,000 homeless, and cost the infrastructure of Zimbabwe and Mozambique $1 billion in damage (Wamukonya and Rukato 2001).The two most recent tropical cyclones to hit Zimbabwe, storms Idai in 2019 and Dineo in 2017, both produced flooding and left a trail of destruction in villages, creating socioeconomic problems for the populace.

A great example of political willpower is the implementation of the facilitating statute in Zimbabwe, which backs the conducive setting in which a specialized disaster management agency was formed. The creation of the institutional framework, appropriate policies, and legal necessities is guided by the corporate dedication. As per the National Policy for Civil Protection, every citizen of this nation should do everything possible to prevent or mitigate the impacts of disasters. The Zimbabwe Civil Protection Act of 1989 specifies that local administrations have the responsibility to supervise the effectiveness of nationwide government-initiated hazard reduction efforts via relevant sector Ministries. The Department of Civil Protection is a government entity that is based within the Ministry of Local Government, Public Works, and Government Housing. Its purpose is to handle large-scale disasters through coordinating all essential stakeholders. This department is headed by a Director and a Deputy Director, assisted by officers, secretaries, typists, and a driver. The current system utilizes the already-existing public, commercial, and non-profit institutions whose regular operations include elements for disaster risk reduction and community growth. Factors such as the absence of a national-level plan policy, lack of central, state or district institutional framework, inadequate inter-sectoral coordination, deficiency of an early warning system, delayed response from relief groups, scarcity of trained and dedicated search and rescue teams, and poor community empowerment have hindered response efforts in past disasters.

Climate change effects are increasingly impacting societies across the globe in unprecedented ways. Felbermayr and Gröschl (2014) maintain that these consequences can lead to a 6.8% decline in per capita GDP during the year they occur, while Klomp and Valckx (2014) describe how climate change negatively influences economic growth per capita using 750 empirical estimates. Munich Re (2018) states that tropical cyclones accounted for over half of global natural disaster losses, with damages from these storms surpassing USD 2111 billion from 1980 to 2018. They are among the most severe natural disasters worldwide (Kunze 2021). Moreover, no region of the African continent has been spared from harm, especially the area south of the Sahara. The majority of countries are more susceptible to natural disasters due to their geographical positioning (Klomp and Valckx 2014). Arndt et al. (2012) reveal that Mozambique’s average yearly rainfall totals have decreased, while the country’s temperatures are believed to have risen by 0.6 °C between 1960 and 2009 due to climate change. In March and April 2019, over 1.7 million people were affected by two devastating cyclones that struck Mozambique in rapid succession. These cyclones resulted in losses and damages of USD 3 billion, with an estimated overall recovery and reconstruction cost of USD 3.4 billion (Mozambican Government, 2019).

Zimbabwe has not been spared from the immense destruction and displacement caused by cyclones, which often lead to natural calamities like tropical cyclones, droughts, and floods. It is anticipated that these hazards will become more frequent and intense because of climate change. Cyclone-induced flooding accounts for more than half of all fatalities from natural disasters, making it one of the most common and deadly events (Rana and Routray 2018; Mhlanga et al. 2019). In 2000, floods resulting from Cyclone Eline caused the deaths of over 700 people, left more than 500,000 homeless, and cost Zimbabwe and Mozambique $1 billion in infrastructure damages (Wamukonya and Rukato 2001). The two most recent tropical cyclones to impact Zimbabwe, Idai in 2019 and Dineo in 2017, both caused flooding and left destruction in their wake in villages, generating socioeconomic challenges for the population.

turns.speaker: user
assistant
turns.text:
Chimanimani has continually faced difficulties in disaster management. The climate in the Horn of Africa (HOA) resembles other arid areas. Vulnerable communities in dry regions experience substantial and unpredictable weather variation, among other obstacles that frequently result in disaster. Though considerable humanitarian aid and development initiatives have been provided, the traditional coping abilities and resilience of these communities remain uncertain. As their socioeconomic status declines, they become more susceptible to risk. National and international actors have recently raised awareness of the issue of climate change and its repercussions in international forums and organizations. This change is portrayed as a significant challenge to development efforts and a reason for increasingly severe and frequent disasters. Various coping and survival strategies have been implemented for an array of climatic and non-climatic reasons to adapt to harsh conditions, but these approaches now appear to be reaching their limits. Adaptation to climate change has recently emerged as a new "BUZZ" or buzzword in the humanitarian and development fields. Consequently, other disaster risk and vulnerability factors may be overlooked. As such, this study examined and scrutinized the limitations of Chimanimani's disaster management capabilities.
